title: Salmon and Dill Pie
subtitle: Flaked salmon in a dill and lemon cream sauce under golden puff pastry
excerpt: Salmon and dill pie is a lighter fish pie with a clean, bright flavour. The salmon is poached briefly in milk, the milk becomes the sauce, and dill runs through the whole thing. A puff pastry lid makes it quick and reliable.
sourceNotes: Method synthesised from British and Scandinavian fish cookery tradition. Salmon and dill is a pairing documented across British and Nordic recipes from the 19th century onward. Poach-and-sauce method follows the established British fish pie technique from Mrs Beeton and Eliza Acton.

recipe.freezeNotes: Assemble the pie, cool completely, and freeze before baking. Thaw overnight in the fridge, then bake covered at 180°C for 35 minutes, uncovering for the last 10.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the filling up to a day ahead and refrigerate. Top with pastry and bake on the day.

glossaryTerms.definition: To cook food gently in a barely simmering liquid, well below boiling point. Poaching preserves the texture of delicate fish and infuses the cooking liquid with flavour, which can then be used as a sauce base.

content.text: Salmon and dill pie works because the two main flavours are strong and complementary: the rich, fatty fish and the clean, anise-edged herb. The method is the same as any milk-poached fish pie. Poach the salmon, use the milk for the sauce, fold everything together, add pastry. Forty-five minutes from start to finish.

Place the {{salmon-fillet}} salmon fillets in a wide saucepan. Pour over the {{whole-milk}} of poaching milk, add the halved {{shallot}} shallots and the {{bay-leaves}} of bay. Bring to a very gentle simmer and
for 5 to 7 minutes until the salmon just flakes when pressed. Lift out carefully. Strain and reserve the poaching milk.
When the salmon is cool enough to handle, break it into large flakes with your hands. Set aside.

Melt the {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a clean saucepan over a medium heat. Add the {{plain-flour}} of plain flour and stir for 2 minutes. Gradually whisk in the reserved poaching milk in three additions, whisking smooth between each. Bring to a gentle simmer and cook for 5 minutes until the sauce coats the back of a spoon thickly. Stir in the {{double-cream}} of double cream and {{lemon}} lemon juice. Season well with salt and white pepper.
Remove from the heat and stir in the chopped dill. Gently fold in the flaked salmon. Pour into a pie dish and leave to cool.
Heat the oven to 200°C. Roll the puff pastry to about 3 mm thick and lay over the filled dish. Press the edges to seal, trim, and crimp. Brush with beaten egg. Cut two small slits in the centre.
Bake for 25 to 30 minutes until the pastry is deep golden. Rest for 5 minutes before serving.

items.symptom: Salmon breaks up into tiny pieces
items.cause: The fish was overcooked during poaching, or handled too roughly when folding in.
items.fix: Pull the salmon from the milk the moment it just flakes when pressed; it will continue cooking in the sauce and oven. Fold it in gently with a large spoon, not a whisk.

items.cause: Dill was added too early and lost its flavour to heat.
items.fix: Always add the dill off the heat, after the sauce has cooked. Its flavour is volatile and fades quickly with prolonged cooking.

items.symptom: Sauce is too thin after baking
items.cause: The roux wasn't cooked long enough before the milk went in, or too much milk was used.
items.fix: Cook the roux for a full 2 minutes before adding any liquid. The finished sauce should hold its shape on a spoon before the salmon is folded in.

items.cause: The salmon released extra water during baking.
items.fix: Make the sauce slightly thicker than seems necessary. Patting the poached salmon dry briefly before folding it in also helps.

content.text: Salmon and dill is a pairing found across British and Scandinavian cooking. In British fish cooking, dill largely replaced fennel as an accompaniment to salmon; both have an anise character, but dill is softer in cream sauces. The pie version is a practical weeknight dish, not a grand Victorian production. It works because the two flavours are strong enough to carry the dish without any additions.
